findings.message: Unbounded full-table scan: `select(Item.id)` carries no LIMIT and no offset, so every row's id is fetched on every request. The sibling endpoint `/items` is guarded by MAX_PAGE_SIZE=100; this one is not. On a table with millions of rows this materialises the entire id column into Python memory and saturates the connection for the duration.
findings.suggestion: Add `.limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)` to the id query as a minimum backstop — or, better, collapse the whole function into `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)))`, which is a single bounded query.

findings.message: N+1 query: after fetching all ids in one query (line 44), `session.get(Item, iid)` inside the loop issues a separate `SELECT * FROM items WHERE id = ?` for every id. Total round-trips = T+1 where T is the number of rows returned by the id query. The session is held open across all T+1 round-trips, blocking a connection-pool slot for the entire duration.
findings.suggestion: Replace the entire body with a single projection query: `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id).limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)))`. This is one round-trip, fetches only the `name` column, and stays within the page-size cap.
